common_name: Peter Pan Agapanthus (Dwarf Blue Agapanthus, Dwarf Lily of the Nile)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Peter Pan is drought-tolerant once established and actually blooms more prolifically when slightly root-bound — avoid dividing too frequently and allow plants to form mature, congested clumps before dividing.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Peter Pan is the most widely planted dwarf agapanthus cultivar — a compact, clump-forming evergreen perennial reaching only 12–18 inches tall in the foliage mass, with flower scapes rising to 24–30 inches above the clump in summer and bearing clusters of clear blue-lavender, trumpet-shaped flowers that are highly attractive to hummingbirds and bees. The compact size makes Peter Pan suitable for situations where the larger agapanthus species would be overwhelming — edging, containers, mass ground cover plantings under trees, and low borders. In zone 9, Peter Pan blooms reliably in June–August and the strap-like, dark-green, evergreen foliage provides attractive year-round ground cover. The key cultural insight is that agapanthus blooms most prolifically when the roots are somewhat constrained — plants that are allowed to fill their container or planting space without division for several years produce the heaviest flowering. Peter Pan is drought-tolerant once established, tolerates reflected heat from paving and walls, and requires minimal maintenance beyond occasional division every 5–7 years when the clump becomes overcrowded. Division is best done in late winter in zone 9.

common_name: Ellamae Agapanthus (Large Blue Lily of the Nile)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Ellamae is a large-growing agapanthus that benefits from ample space to form broad clumps — allow 24–30 inches between plants at planting, and resist dividing until the clump is truly overcrowded and bloom quality declines.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Ellamae is a robust, large-growing agapanthus cultivar that is one of the most widely grown and best-performing selections for warm-climate gardens — producing large, arching clumps of wide, strap-like, dark-green evergreen leaves from which tall (4–5 foot) flowering scapes rise in summer, each bearing large, dense globular heads of deep blue-purple, trumpet-shaped flowers. The large flower heads are spectacular in mass plantings and outstanding as cut flowers — lasting well in arrangements. Ellamae is more cold-hardy than many agapanthus, tolerating brief freezes to about 10–15°F, and it thrives in zone 7–11 as a reliable perennial where it forms ever-expanding clumps over the years. In zone 9, Ellamae blooms from June through August in full sun, with the season extending into September in partial shade. The large, stately habit — with foliage clumps reaching 2–3 feet and flower scapes rising to 4–5 feet — gives it a sculptural, architectural quality exceptional for creating bold masses along fences, walls, and drives. Extremely drought-tolerant once established. Divide only when clumps become overcrowded — large, mature clumps produce the most spectacular flower displays.

common_name: Storm Cloud Agapanthus (Deep Blue-Purple Lily of the Nile)

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Storm Cloud's dark flower color is most intense in full sun — in partial shade the blue-purple color remains attractive but may not achieve the deepest navy tones.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; Storm Cloud agapanthus is a mid-sized cultivar distinguished by unusually dark, deep blue-purple to near-navy flowers — among the darkest flower color available in any common agapanthus selection — produced on upright scapes in midsummer above clumps of narrow, dark-green strap leaves. The intense flower color provides maximum contrast with lighter companion plants — white-flowering perennials, silver-foliage plants, and pale yellow companions create dramatic color combinations with the deep blue-purple Storm Cloud. At 2.5–3 feet tall in flower, it fits mixed perennial borders more easily than the large Ellamae-type selections. Storm Cloud is evergreen in zone 9, providing attractive groundcover foliage year-round, and it is reliably winter-hardy to zone 7 with occasional protection. Like all agapanthus, Storm Cloud is drought-tolerant once established, thrives with minimal fertilization in moderately fertile soils, and actually tends to produce fewer flowers in overly rich soil or with excessive nitrogen fertilization. Outstanding for mass plantings where the deep blue-purple color creates a bold seasonal statement.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Moonbeam performs best in average to lean soils — overly rich, moist soils cause floppy growth and reduced bloom.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Moonbeam was named the Perennial Plant of the Year in 1992 and remains one of the most beloved and widely grown perennials in American horticulture — a threadleaf coreopsis cultivar producing a nearly continuous display of small, daisy-like, soft creamy-lemon yellow flowers from early summer through fall on a tidy, mounding plant with fine, thread-like, dark-green foliage. The soft, pale yellow flower color — cooler and more subtle than the bright golden yellow of most coreopsis — makes Moonbeam an exceptional mixer: it harmonizes with virtually every other flower color, from warm orange and red to cool blue and purple, without dominating or clashing. The fine, feathery foliage texture contrasts beautifully with bold-leafed companions. Moonbeam is highly heat-tolerant and drought-tolerant once established, and the deadheading requirement is minimal — light shearing after the first major flush encourages continued bloom. In zone 9, Moonbeam blooms from May through October in good conditions. Avoid rich, moist soil and excess nitrogen fertilization, which produce lush, floppy growth at the expense of flowers. Excellent for mass planting, edging, and pollinator gardens.
sources: Perennial Plant Association — Perennial Plant of the Year 1992

planting.note: Plant divisions or container plants in spring in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Zagreb is the most compact commonly available threadleaf coreopsis, making it excellent for foreground positions, edging, and container planting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; Zagreb is the most compact and upright of the commonly grown threadleaf coreopsis cultivars — a tidy, mounding plant reaching only 12–18 inches tall and wide, with the same fine, thread-like foliage and long succession of bright golden-yellow daisy flowers as the larger threadleaf selections but in a more restrained package that fits small borders, edging positions, and containers that would be overwhelmed by standard coreopsis. The bright, clear golden-yellow flowers are more saturated in color than the soft Moonbeam, making Zagreb a higher-impact plant for positions where its color can be used as an accent rather than a blender. It blooms reliably from late spring through fall in zone 9, with light shearing after heavy bloom periods encouraging additional flush. Zagreb is exceptional for mass planting along walks, in rock gardens, and as a long-season foreground plant in full-sun mixed borders. Like all threadleaf coreopsis, Zagreb is self-seeding and will naturalize in appropriate conditions — seeds germinate readily and maintain the compact habit characteristic. Extremely low-maintenance, drought-tolerant, and resistant to deer browsing.
